blocks.text: Re: Operating Model Redesign for {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}}
Dear Ms. Gallegos:

This letter records the terms on which we will redesign the operating model of {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}}. It follows our meetings this spring and the materials your office sent afterward. It sets out what we will do, how we will staff it, what we will need from you, and the commercial terms that govern the work. Once you have countersigned and returned it, this letter is the agreement between us and it supersedes anything said before it.
Scope of the Engagement

The engagement addresses how the company is organized to run: its reporting structure, the way decisions are made and by whom, the accountabilities that sit with each layer, and the cost that structure carries. We will produce three things. The first is a diagnostic of the current operating model, built from your organization charts, your management accounts, and interviews with the leaders who run the business day to day. The second is a target operating model: a structure, a set of decision rights, and the governance to support them, designed for where you are taking the company, not for where it has been. The third is an implementation roadmap that sequences the change and identifies where the return is largest.

Certain matters sit outside this engagement and are available separately if you want them. We will recommend changes to roles and headcount; we will not carry them out, and the decisions on people remain yours. Systems selection and implementation, tax structuring, and legal reorganization are not within our scope. Where our work touches a question we are not engaged to answer, we will say so and stop, instead of guessing at it.
How We Will Work

We begin with your own figures and your own charts, not with a template carried in from another company. In the first weeks we will map the current structure, interview the leadership team, and build a cost baseline that ties to your management accounts, so that every number we later put in front of you can be traced back to one you already recognize. From that base we design the target model and test it against the demands the business will place on it. We bring a point of view and we will state it plainly; we do not hand you a menu of options and ask you to do the designing.
The Team

Jason Torres, Principal, leads the engagement and is your day-to-day contact. He is responsible for the design work and for what we present to you. Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant, leads the cost and organization analysis and will run the data exchange with your finance staff; she is the person to reach on the management accounts and the reconciliations. I review the analysis and the recommendation before either reaches you, as I do with everything that leaves this office. We do not change the people on your engagement without telling you first.
Fees and Commencement

Our fee and the basis on which it is calculated are set out on the signature page, which our respective counsel have executed separately, and they are not restated here. We invoice monthly for the work performed in the preceding month, payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Work outside the scope described above is not undertaken on assumption; if you ask for something this letter does not cover, we will quote it and wait for your written authorization before we begin. Work commences on {{fact:f:E-2008-001.start}}, subject to your countersignature and to our receiving the current organization charts, the last two years of management accounts, and access to the leaders we will need to interview.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on the date of your signature below and continues until the roadmap has been delivered and our final invoice paid. Either party may end it on thirty days' written notice to the other. On termination you will pay us for the work performed and the expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will deliver our work product in the state it then stands, with a written note of what remains unfinished. An unfinished diagnostic is not a recommendation and should not be relied on as one.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence and use it only for this engagement. Access is limited to the team named above and to those in our office whose work requires it. We will not disclose your information to anyone outside the firm without your written consent, except where a court or a regulator with jurisdiction compels it, and in that event we will tell you first if we are free to. Our work product is prepared for your management and is not to be used on another matter without our written consent. This obligation survives the end of the engagement.
Limitation of Liability

Our liability to you arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract or in tort, will not exceed the total fees you pay us under it, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct. Neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, whether or not the possibility was known. Our analysis rests on the accuracy of the information you furnish; where that information proves materially wrong, the consequences of the error are not ours to carry, though we will tell you promptly when we see reason to doubt what we have been given.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Before either of us commences proceedings, we will attempt to resolve any dispute by discussion between senior representatives of the two parties. This letter and its signature page are the entire agreement between us on their subject, and either may be amended only in writing signed by both of us.

If these terms reflect your understanding, please sign both copies where indicated, keep one for your file, and return the other to this office. If any part of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign. We are glad to be doing this work with Hughes Group, and we will be ready to start the interviews as soon as the letter is back.
Very truly yours,

To: Brenda Rodriguez, Jeffrey Jackson
From: Jason Torres
Re: {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}} operating model redesign, kickoff and staffing

The letter is countersigned and we are on the clock as of {{fact:f:E-2008-001.start}}. The job is to redesign how {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}} is organized to run and to hand the client a structure, a set of decision rights, and a roadmap they can act on. Eight months, three deliverables: a diagnostic of the current model, a target operating model, and an implementation roadmap. We build the case from the client's own charts and their own numbers, so that when we recommend a change the evidence for it is theirs and not ours. Our client contact is Linda Gallegos, the CFO, and she is the single door. Everything that goes to the client goes through Linda Gallegos, and nobody on this team calls a business-unit head without her knowing first.

A word on the boundary, because it will be tested. We recommend on people; we do not decide on them, and we do not carry out headcount actions. Systems, tax, and legal restructuring are outside this engagement. If any of that gets asked for in a corridor, the answer is that we will scope it, not that we have started it.
Lines of Work

items: Current-state map. Brenda Rodriguez owns the structure and the decision-rights map: who reports where, who decides what, and where the same decision is made twice. Build it from the charts and the interviews, not from what people say the chart is supposed to be.

Cost baseline. Brenda Rodriguez ties the cost of the current structure to the management accounts, by function and by layer. If a number does not tie, that gap is a finding, and I want it flagged in the first pass, not buried in the third.
Target model design. I own this. Structure, decision rights, and the governance to hold them, built for where the business is going. Nothing here reaches the client until the diagnostic supports it.
Roadmap and sequencing. I own this with Brenda Rodriguez. Which changes come first, what each one returns, and what it takes to land it.
Staffing and schedule. Jeffrey Jackson has us covered through the diagnostic. If the interview load grows past what we planned, tell Jeffrey Jackson before it becomes a scheduling problem, not after.

Client cadence. I own the line to the client. A written status goes to Linda Gallegos every two weeks, and it goes out even in the weeks when the news is thin, because a client who hears from us only when there is good news stops trusting the quiet weeks.

blocks.text: This Week

items: Brenda Rodriguez drafts the data request, itemized, with the account mapping we need and the field definitions attached, and I send it to Linda Gallegos by Thursday.
We agree the interview list, name by name, before we ask the client for a single calendar slot, so we are not renegotiating access once the on-site weeks have started.
Jeffrey Jackson confirms the rooms and the schedule for the on-site weeks and holds a half-day of slack for the interviews that always slip.
Nobody quotes a structure or a number to anyone at Hughes Group until the baseline reconciles.

blocks.text: One thing I need settled by Friday: the data request and the interview list, both agreed and both out the door to Linda Gallegos. We do not want to start the on-site work negotiating access we should have locked down this week.
Linda Gallegos, with Brenda Rodriguez copied:

A short status and one request. We have the organization charts and the first set of management accounts, and Brenda Rodriguez has started reconciling the cost of the current structure against them. The interviews with your business-unit heads are set for the next two weeks. That is where most of the diagnostic on {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}} will be decided, so please hold those slots even if the week gets busy.

The one thing we are missing is the management accounts broken out by cost center instead of by legal entity. Brenda Rodriguez can work from either, but the cost-center cut lets us tie spend to the structure instead of to the balance sheet, which is the whole point of the baseline. If your finance team can send that by the end of the week, we stay on schedule.
Nothing else needed from you today. I will send the interview list tomorrow with the times, so your assistants can put them on calendars.
Jason Torres
Brenda Rodriguez, copying Linda Gallegos:

The cost-center accounts came in overnight, so we can start the baseline properly. Before the on-site week, tie the payroll and the contractor spend to the structure by layer, not only by function; the layer view is what shows where the decisions and the cost have drifted apart. And flag any cost center that does not map cleanly to a box on the current chart. Those are usually where the interesting questions sit.

Linda, thank you for turning the accounts around so quickly. On {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}} we are now where we wanted to be at this point: charts in, accounts in, interviews set. The interview list and times will reach your office today, and Brenda Rodriguez will send your finance team a short list of definitions so we are all counting the same things.
If anything on the list looks wrong to you, say so before the first session and we will fix it then. Otherwise we are in good shape for the on-site weeks.
Jason Torres
Operating Model Redesign for {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}}

items: A progress briefing for Linda Gallegos, Chief Financial Officer
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2008-001.start}}
Presented by Jason Torres, Principal
Prepared by the engagement team for the client's review

blocks.text: What We Were Asked To Do

items: Redesign how the company is organized to run: structure, decision rights, and the cost that structure carries
Diagnose the current operating model from the charts, the management accounts, and the leadership interviews
Design a target model built for where the business is going, not for where it has been
Lay out a roadmap that sequences the change and shows where the return is largest

blocks.text: How We Are Working

items: We start from your own charts and management accounts, not from a template
Brenda Rodriguez leads the cost baseline and the current-state map
Interviews with the business-unit heads run through the on-site weeks
The cost baseline is built by function and by layer, so spend can be read against the structure that drives it
Every number we show you ties back to one your finance team already recognizes

blocks.text: What We Are Hearing

items: Leaders describe the same decision being made in more than one place, and then reconciled later
The cost of coordinating across the structure shows up as meetings and rework, not as a line on the accounts
Accountability that is clearly held moves work faster than accountability shared across several roles
These are themes from the interviews so far, offered as observations and not yet as findings

blocks.text: Where the Work Stands

items: Organization charts and management accounts received and reconciled
Cost baseline for Hughes Group built by function; the layer view is in progress
Leadership interviews under way, roughly half complete
Early pattern: decisions and cost do not sit in the same place, and we are testing how far that goes
No target structure is on the table yet, and none should be inferred from this briefing

blocks.text: What Comes Next

items: Finish the interviews and close the cost baseline
Frame two or three target-model options for discussion, not for decision
Bring the diagnostic findings to the next working session
Questions to Jason Torres; data questions direct to Brenda Rodriguez

blocks.text: Working Session: Cost Baseline and Current-State Findings
Minutes recorded by Brenda Rodriguez. Subject: {{fact:f:E-2008-001.client}} operating model redesign, working session with the client.
Attendees

items: Jason Torres, Principal
Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant
Linda Gallegos, Chief Financial Officer, Hughes Group

blocks.text: Cost Baseline

I walked the table through the cost baseline as it stands. The finding is that the current structure carries more layers than the headcount justifies: in two functions there are five reporting layers between the CFO's office and the front line where three would carry the same work. That is a measurement from the charts and the payroll file, and it holds. What it means for cost is a judgment I am not ready to put a number on, because about a fifth of the contractor spend is booked to cost centers that do not map cleanly to a box on the chart, and until that maps I would be guessing at the denominator. Jason Torres asked whether the unmapped spend changes the shape of the finding or only its size. On the evidence so far it changes the size, not the shape, but I logged that as an assumption to confirm, not a conclusion. The reconciliation against the management accounts ties at the function level; it is the allocation within two functions that is still soft, and I will hold that number back a week. Publishing an allocation I already expect to change would only put a wrong figure into circulation.
Where Decisions Sit

We reviewed the decision-rights map against the reporting structure. The pattern is consistent with the cost finding: several decisions the chart places at the business-unit head are in practice made two layers up, and a smaller number that should sit centrally are made in the units without a common rule. Linda Gallegos confirmed the second point from her own experience with the capital approvals, where two units apply different thresholds for the same kind of spend, and added two examples we had not captured. I noted both for the map. Jason Torres asked whether the drift is deliberate or accidental; the honest answer is that we do not know yet, and the interviews are where that gets settled. None of this is a recommendation; it is the current state, described, and it needs the remaining interviews before we test any change against it.
Open Questions

Two questions are open and both bear on the schedule. The first is the unmapped contractor spend, which finance is tracing. The second is whether the two functions with the extra layers are the exception or the rule across the company; the interviews still outstanding are the ones that will settle it. Linda Gallegos asked that we not characterize the structure to the business-unit heads before those interviews, so the sessions stay diagnostic and do not turn into negotiations. We agreed.
Action Items

rows: Trace the unmapped contractor spend to cost centers and remap it to the chart
Extend the layer analysis to the remaining functions once the mapping is clean
Complete the outstanding leadership interviews and log the decision-rights gaps
Hold the current-state findings inside the team until the interviews are complete
